Interviewer: Can you walk me through a challenging system design problem you solved recently?

Candidate: Sure. At my last role, we had a service that was falling over under load during peak
traffic. I started by profiling the request path and found that a synchronous call to a third-party
API was blocking the entire request pipeline. I proposed moving that call to an async queue with a
worker pool, which meant the primary request path could return quickly with a "processing" status and
poll or webhook back when the third-party call completed. We used Celery with Redis as the broker,
similar to a pattern I've used elsewhere.

Interviewer: How did you handle failures in that async path?

Candidate: We added retry with exponential backoff, a dead-letter queue for anything that failed
after the max retry count, and an alert that fired if the dead-letter queue grew past a threshold.
We also made sure the operation was idempotent so retries were safe - we used the resource's own ID
plus an operation type as an idempotency key.

Interviewer: What would you do differently if you were starting over?

Candidate: I'd probably introduce the async boundary earlier, before it became an emergency. I'd also
push for better observability from day one - we ended up building most of our tracing after the fact,
which made the initial debugging much slower than it needed to be.
